ACE Illinois successfully delivered a productive week-long program 
that brought together over 65 global and regional leaders from the 
Americas and beyond. The program took place in Chicago, Champaign, 
Urbana, Joliet, Naperville and Peoria within the state of Illinois, United 
States from April 27 to May 2, 2025. ACE participants engaged with 
Illinois’ vibrant industries, including Life Science, Quantum Computing, 
Energy Production, Advanced Manufacturing, Agri-Tech, Life Sciences, 
Transportation and Logistics.
Over 560 opportunities for collaboration were registered in the final ACE 
Illinois survey including potential trade and investment partnerships, 
new Research and Development (R&D) projects, start-up soft-landing, 
knowledge exchange, and business expansion, among others. Participants 
identified cooperation opportunities with Illinois’ vibrant ecosystem and 
learned from good practices and models on economic development. 
Americas Competitiveness Exchange (ACE) is a powerful mechanism 
for fostering cooperation and forming new partnerships. According to 
data from the ACE Illinois final survey, 95% of the global and regional 
leaders expressed that ACE Illinois exceeded or greatly exceeded their 
expectations (48 surveyed).

THE ACE PROGRAM
Americas
Competitiveness
Exchange
Created in 2014, the Americas Competitiveness Exchange (ACE) program 
is jointly implemented and supported by the General Secretariat of 
the Organization of American States (OAS) and the Government of 
the United States, through the U.S. Department of Commerce, U.S. 
Economic Development Administration (EDA), and the Department of 
State (Permanent Mission to the OAS). The commitment of the OAS 
and the United States Government to ACE extends from the design 
and implementation phases to evaluation, monitoring, and reporting in 
collaboration with host countries and regions. The ACE initiative fosters 
an environment for establishing long-term partnerships, cooperation 
opportunities, and new projects, making it a network of networks and 
a community of impact. With the implementation of 20 ACE Editions, 
ACE has evolved into a flagship hemispheric program, providing tangible 
long-term benefits to countries and communities involved.

ACE is the premier economic development, innovation, 
and entrepreneurial network in the Americas, earning 
recognition as one of the OAS main high-level events. 
ACE operates as a core initiative of the Inter-American 
Competitiveness Network (RIAC), with the OAS acting as 
the RIAC Technical Secretariat. RIAC is a regional platform 
that gathers high-level authorities and key stakeholders 
responsible 
for 
competitiveness, 
innovation, 
and 
economic development in all the active OAS Member 
States and the Organization’s 71 Permanent Observers.

WHAT IS ACE?
The Objectives

ACE ILLINOIS
THE ACE ILLINOIS AGENDA 
The program took place in Chicago, Champaign, Urbana, Joliet, Naperville and 
Peoria within the state of Illinois, United States from April 27 to May 2, 2025. 
The high-level delegation connected with over 20 sites during the one-week 
program to learn good practices and enhance collaboration opportunities on 
economic development, innovation, and entrepreneurship.
https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLCFmKggkdz0EUiEx3nM202wn258DgZ7Ce
Day 1, ACE Illinois, 
Monday, April 28, 2025
Day 2, ACE Illinois, 
Tuesday, April 29, 2025
Day 3, ACE Illinois, 
Wednesday, April 30, 2025
Day 4, ACE Illinois, 
Thursday May 1, 2025
Report, 
ACE Illinois

Monday, April 28
ACE Illinois commenced its highly anticipated program bringing 
together economic competitiveness leaders, innovators, and 
entrepreneurs, from 20 countries of the Americas and beyond for 
a day of dialogue, knowledge-sharing and connection.
The day began with the opening ceremony led by Kristin Richards, 
Director of the Illinois Department of Commerce and Economic 
Opportunity. 
 
Opening remarks from the University of Illinois System and the 
Organization of American States highlighted the essential role 
of public – private-academic partnerships in shaping the future 
of our cities, states, and countries through research, policy, and 
innovation.
The first stop of the program took delegates to the UIC Innovation 
Center, a space for interdisciplinary innovation and education. Its 
mission is to channel scientific research capabilities into actual 
solutions to improve health outcomes. 
UIC fosters experiential learning for students, engages in 
close collaboration with private sector partners, and advances 
translational research efforts to generate human-centered 
projects with global reach.
 
A panel titled ‘How Innovation Powers Health Progress’ brought 
together experts to explore the intersection of healthcare, 
technology, and human-centered design. Top UIC researchers 
and program directors shared key projects related to health 
innovation where collaboration opportunities are possible with 
the ACE Delegation. 
Interactive tours of the center offered delegates an inside look 
at innovative multi-disciplinary approaches shaping the future of 
health and wellness.
 
The second stop of the day was MxD—where manufacturing 
meets digital innovation. In partnership with the United States 
Department of Defense, the 22,000 square-foot future factory 
tests and demonstrates new technology, train workforce, and 
deploys cybersecurity tools for manufacturing businesses, 
Discovering Illinois with the ACE Delegation

including small and medium-sized enterprises. This state-of-the-
art center features some of the most advanced manufacturing 
equipment and resources in partnership with over 600 private 
sector partners. 
Delegates were welcomed by Bernardino Baratta, CEO of 
MxD who offered insight into MxD’s mission of equipping U.S. 
manufacturers with the tools and expertise needed to embrace 
digital transformation. Delegates then toured the facility to 
witness its smart manufacturing systems in action. 
 
In the afternoon, the delegation visited 1871, Chicago’s premier 
tech incubator located in the historic Merchandise Mart. 1871 
is a non-profit featuring founders, visionary leaders, resilient 
innovators, and steadfast supporters in the tech community of 
Illinois. Offering programs for early stage, growth & late-stage 
startups, and corporate innovators, 1871 inspires, equips, and 
support tech businesses. Since its launch in 2012, 1871has raised 
$3.5B in venture capital and created over 15,000 jobs. 
A panel moderated by Jaime Di Paulo, President & CEO of the 
Illinois Hispanic Chamber of Commerce centered on the role of 
community connectors, bridging industries and empowering local 
economies. One of the regional priorities to unlock Chicago and 
the Midwest’s innovation potential as a global hub for investment 
and innovation is for the region to become the Quantum-capital 
of the United States.
Delegates also visited MATTER— a global healthcare startup 
incubator building innovation with impact. Matter mentors’ 
entrepreneurs to accelerate their businesses and fast-forward 
solutions through technology and collaboration. Since its 
inception in 2015, MATTER has supported over 1,000 healthcare 
and life sciences startups, fostering a community of 60,000 
entrepreneurs, executives, investors, and experts dedicated to 
improving health and healthcare. Collaborating with dozens 
of hospitals, health systems, universities, and industry-leading 
companies, MATTER accelerates innovation, advances care and 
transforms lives.
Day one concluded with a networking reception—offering an 
opportunity for delegates to reflect, connect, and share ideas 
sparked by the day’s program.

Tuesday, April 29
Day two of ACE Illinois took delegates to the University of Illinois 
Research Park in Urbana-Champaign, where groundbreaking 
innovation, academic excellence, and industry partnerships 
intersect.
Immersive visits to the Integrated Bioprocessing Research Lab 
-IBRL-, the Food Science and Human Nutrition Pilot Processing 
Plant, and the iFAB Tech Hub—showcased how cutting-edge 
facilities support advanced research and commercialization in 
bioprocessing and agri-food systems. These pilot-scale facilities 
are designed to test and accelerate new technologies and 
solutions as part of the IFAB – Illinois Fermentation and Agriculture 
Biomanufacturing Hub. These leading initiatives provide advanced 
fermentation infrastructure to de-risk innovative ventures at a 
commercially relevant scale.
A panel discussion on ‘University-Driven Innovation and 
Entrepreneurship’ followed, highlighting the ways in which 
the University of Illinois is fueling talent development, startup 
creation, and economic transformation in the region.
ACE leaders explored the 88,000-square-foot facility that houses 
state of the art computing, networking, and data systems. This 
infrastructure and leading scientists are building cutting edge 
applications for AI and quantum technologies in collaboration with 
private sector and other research partners in Illinois and across the 
country. The facility offers top-flight physical and cybersecurity, 
including monitoring and tracking of network flows.
Researchers at the Center for Advanced Bioenergy and 
Bioproducts Innovation –CABBI-, shared projects advancing 
solutions for innovative fuels, resilience and advanced materials 
through plant biology and engineering.
CABBI is a collaboration between the University of Illinois 
Urbana-Champaign and 20 partner institutions. It integrates 
recent advances in agronomics, genomics, and synthetic and 
computational biology to increase the value of energy crops — 
using a “plants as factories” approach to grow fuels and chemicals.
In the afternoon, ACE delegation engaged with the Holonyak 
Micro and Nanotechnology Lab, one of the country’s largest and 
most sophisticated university facilities for conducting photonics,

microelectronics, biotechnology, and nanotechnology research. 
Leaders from the Illinois Quantum and Microelectronics Park— Dr. 
Susan A. Martinis, Brian DeMarco and Harley Johnson—delivered 
an in-depth presentation on Illinois’ rapidly expanding quantum 
ecosystem, underscoring the state’s emerging leadership in 
quantum information science and advanced microelectronics.
The next stop was Distillery Labs, a vibrant new incubator in 
Peoria, a regional innovation hub supporting entrepreneurship 
through collaboration, technology, and community engagement. 
To conclude the day, the Innovation Exchange Coalition and 
Distillery Labs hosted an Innovation Startup Showcase, featuring 
eight startup companies: SmartCharts, Manuar, NXT7, Healp, 
PowerBox Technology, DigiFarmz, TredBase, and Humod.
Delegates engaged with founders and innovators, discovering 
how startups across Illinois are transforming ideas into scalable 
solutions that drive local and global businesses with impact.
Day 2 of ACE Illinois reinforced the state’s deep commitment to 
innovation at every level. As the week continues, so too does our 
shared pursuit of knowledge sharing, collaboration, and economic 
development.

Wednesday, April 30
Day three of ACE Illinois brought the spotlight to regional 
collaboration, workforce innovation, and economic planning—
showcasing how local solutions can power global connections.
The day began with presentations from Winton & Strawn LLP, 
an international law firm headquartered in Chicago. Delegates 
participated in insightful discussions focused on doing business 
and investing in Illinois and the United States.
The Chicago Metropolitan Agency for Planning –CMAP- is 
a regional government agency that works on behalf of the 
seven counties, 284 municipalities, and 8.6 million residents 
of Northeastern Illinois. Through the CMAP and its Data 
Hub, communities access data snapshots that inform their 
comprehensive planning to optimize the regional transportation 
infrastructure and maximize economic competitiveness drivers 
for today and tomorrow.
Panels on regional transportation and economic development 
strategies highlighted how cross-collaboration within counties 
has increased the visibility and positive outcomes for all. Panelists 
agreed on the benefit of working together to market as a region 
to reach a more impactful national and international scale. 
Following the sessions, delegates were guided through the historic 
Old Chicago Post Office— an iconic symbol of urban revitalization, 
where history meets forward-looking redevelopment.
Next, at the Richard J. Daley Community College, an institution 
serving approximately 12,000 students annually, local leaders 
shared their main programs focused on workforce development 
for digital and manufacturing jobs. 
ACE leaders learned about the “Make It In Illinois” statewide 
campaign aimed at growing the manufacturing workforce talent 
pool and increasing enrollment in training.
Dr. Janine Janosky, President of Daley College, presented 
collaboration opportunities and good practices building on the 
experiences of the college serving students through deep-rooted 
partnerships with community organizations and industries.

Delegates then engaged with tech teams at the state-of-the-
art Manufacturing Technology and Engineering Center—also 
known as MTEC—a facility that exemplifies the future of technical 
education and workforce readiness. The MTEC trains with over 
1,000 students annually. A Center of Excellence was designed 
to ensure a quality curriculum in high demands fields, such as 
advanced manufacturing. 
Jim Longino of the Greater Southwest Development Corporation 
spoke about the role of community development organizations 
in 
fostering 
international 
engagement 
and 
cross-cultural 
partnerships.
The day concluded with a visit to The Hatchery, a non-profit food 
and beverage incubator located in Chicago’s East Garfield Park 
neighborhood. It is dedicated to helping local entrepreneurs build 
and grow successful businesses. Through cutting edge commercial 
kitchen facilities, robust entrepreneurship curriculums, and 
partnership with corporate sponsors and foundations, The 
Hatchery supports local food and beverage entrepreneurs, 
cultivates local job opportunities, and accelerates local economic 
growth.
The Hatchery supports job creation, economic mobility, and 
culinary innovation in the city’s West Side. Delegates had the 
opportunity to experience firsthand the innovation and community 
impact taking place within this collaborative space. 
From economic planning, transportation policy, to workforce 
development and innovation. Day 3 of ACE Illinois emphasized the 
importance of community-driven approaches to fueling progress.

Thursday, May 1
On Day Four of ACE Illinois, delegates explored the dynamic 
intersection 
of 
logistics, 
sustainability, 
and 
community 
development.
The day began with a visit to the CenterPoint Intermodal 
Center at Will County, America’s largest master-planned inland 
port. Located 40 miles southwest of Chicago, it is strategically 
positioned at the epicenter of the region’s transportation 
infrastructure. This facility stretches over 6,400 acres and creates 
20.000 jobs. The campus has warehouse and distribution center 
solutions, with rail access, interline, and carload services for 
international and domestic intermodal capacity featuring a Free 
Trade Zone. ACE leaders learned about the critical role of Will 
County in national supply chains and global trade.
At Joliet Junior College, delegates engaged in a discussion 
focused on ‘Why Will County Works’, —highlighting the region’s 
strategic assets, workforce readiness, and collaborative approach 
to driving business success.
The delegation then traveled to Naperville, a community known 
for its blend of innovation, education, and quality of life.
Delegates visited Ecolab, a global sustainability leader offering 
water management solutions and services. Ecolab engages 
with more than 40 industries in 170 countries by providing 
comprehensive science-based solutions, data-driven insights, 
and world-class service to advance growth, maintain clean and 
safe environments, and optimize water conservation and energy 
use. During the visit, leaders from the Americas and beyond 
explored cutting-edge technologies that help minimize water 
usage while maximizing operational results.
The next stop was Naper Settlement and the Birck Family 
Innovation Gateway, a vibrant space where history informs and 
inspires community innovation.
ACE leaders were greeted by Mayor Scott A. Wehrli, who 
highlighted 
the 
values 
and 
community-driven 
economic 
development approach of the City of Naperville.

A featured presentation, ‘Understanding the Naperville Riverwalk’, 
showcased the transformative power of civic engagement and 
the visionary planning that transformed the Riverwalk into a 
celebrated destination.
The day concluded with a networking reception, where delegates 
reflected on the insights gained and continued to build meaningful 
relationships and achieve important results in ACE Illinois.
Day Four of ACE Illinois highlighted the depth of regional 
leadership—and the collective momentum shaping the future of 
economic growth.

Friday, May 2
Day Five of ACE Illinois began with a celebration of international 
partnership and diplomacy. Hosted at the Consulate General of 
Canada in Chicago, delegates were welcomed by Consul General 
Madeleine Féquière for a morning of dialogue and collaboration—
affirming the enduring ties between nations through economic 
diplomacy and shared innovation goals.
The next stop took the delegation to the Discovery Partners 
Institute, a transformative initiative of the University of Illinois 
System. DPI is a hub for cultivating tech talent, advancing applied 
research, and strengthening Illinois’ innovation economy. 
Venkat Venkatakrishnan, Director of Research, welcomed the 
ACE delegation and opened a series of compelling presentations. 
Sessions explored The Value of Data in Evidence-Based Policy 
Making and a forward-looking Comprehensive Economic 
Development Strategy—underscoring the importance of informed 
decision-making in shaping resilient economies.
Delegates then visited mHUB, Chicago’s premier innovation center 
for physical product development and advanced manufacturing.
Guided tours showcased mHUB’s brand-new 80,000 square foot 
facility—opened in 2023—designed to anchor the revitalization of 
Chicago’s near West Side and serve as a catalyst for job creation 
and economic development.
To finalize the week, delegates gathered at the Metropolitan Club 
for a formal closing ceremony—reflecting on the highlights and 
shared discoveries of ACE Illinois. 
The event concluded with 
the official transition to the 
next host of the Americas 
Competitiveness 
Exchange. 
ACE Belem, in the Brazilian 
state of Pará, was announced 
as the next host of ACE in Fall 
2025—continuing the legacy 
of regional collaboration and 
innovation exchange.

BY THE NUMBERS1
560+ Opportunities for cooperation registered 
in the final survey.
65 Leaders were part of the ACE Illinois 
Delegation, including participants, special guests, 
local organizers, and ACE Committee Members.
20 Countries: 17 OAS Member States 
and 3 OAS Permanent Observers.
40+ Speakers shared their knowledge and 
offered opportunities of cooperation.
35+ Sites and Projects were featured in 
ACE Illinois.
100% of survey respondents (48 participants) 
would recommend the ACE
program to other leaders.
95% of survey respondents (47 participants) 
said that ACE exceeded or greatly exceeded their 
expectations.
1. Data reflects information extracted from responses to the Final Survey received as of June, 2025. Out of the 48 total participants 
surveyed, 48 responded to the Final Survey, which accounts for 100% of participants.

ILLINOIS OVERVIEW: 
KEY CLUSTERS AND ECONOMIC LANDSCAPE
The State of Illinois offers an unparalleled opportunity at the epicenter of North 
America. Illinois provides companies with a world class business environment, 
a central location with easy access for any operation, a well-educated and 
experienced workforce, and an opportunity to be an integral part of the second 
most economically powerful region in the country.
 
Illinois is also one of the world’s most culturally vibrant states and has become 
a magnet for the country’s brightest minds. Businesses can benefit from low 
corporate taxes and tax-free personal property that help make Illinois one of the 
most business-friendly places in the nation.
# 1 State in the Midwest for business creation 
 # 1 Metro for new & expanding companies 
 # 1 State for sustainable development 
 # 5 Highest GDP in the nation 
 # 18 Largest economy in the world 
What Sets Illinois Apart?

FEATURED INDUSTRY CLUSTERS 
Advanced Manufacturing 
Life Sciences
Next Generation Agriculture, 
Agriculture Tech and Food 
Processing
Quantum Computing, AI, and 
Microelectronics 
Transportation, 
Distribution, and Logistics 
Energy Production and 
Manufacturing

Advanced 
Manufacturing 
Illinois has a long history in manufacturing, including infrastructure, workforce 
and Research and Development (R&D) capacities. 
Fast-growing sectors include inputs for vehicles, airplanes, drones, energy, and 
life sciences, including machinery manufacturing, chemical manufacturing, 
robotics, and advanced materials, including nanomaterials.
ACE Illinois offered opportunities to advance collaboration and new 
partnerships with:
Manufacturing x Digital (MxD)
22,000 square-foot future factory is used to test and demonstrate new 
technology, train workforces, and demonstrate the need for cybersecurity in 
manufacturing. A state-of-the-art innovation center features some of the most 
advanced manufacturing equipment in the world working with large corporations 
and small businesses.
Richard J. Daley College
Serves approximately 12,000 students annually across our two campuses 
through credit, Adult Education, and Continuing Education programming. The 
Manufacturing Technology & Engineering Center (MTEC) trains students with 
insight from industry partners to ensure a quality curriculum and certifications in 
high demands fields, such as advanced manufacturing.
FEATURED INDUSTRY CLUSTERS

Incubators and Accelerators 
for Start Ups and SMEs
1871 is an accelerator for startups and corporate innovators, which provides 
mentorship, inspires, equips, and support extraordinary tech businesses. Since 
its launch in 2012, this has raised $3.5B in venture capital and created over 14,500 
jobs.
Distillery Labs is a Community Hub providing critical resources for entrepreneurs 
and small businesses in Peoria, Illinois. It features makerspace, offices, commercial 
teaching kitchen, podcast studio, coworking sites, conference rooms, and special 
event space. 
The Hatchery Chicago, through cutting edge commercial kitchen facilities, 
robust entrepreneurship curriculums, and partnership with corporate sponsors 
and foundations, The Hatchery supports local food and beverage entrepreneurs, 
cultivates local job opportunities, and accelerates local economic growth.
mHUB is an innovation center for all things HardTech. It offers prototyping 
labs, industry partnerships, and services designed to remove barriers and 
increase the probability of building a sustainable business to commercialize 
new physical products. This leading innovation center is embedded within the 
most comprehensive manufacturing economy in the U.S. with 12,000+ local 
manufacturers, generating anually $10 billion and employ over 565,000 people.
FEATURED INDUSTRY CLUSTERS

Life 
Sciences
The UIC Innovation Center is a space for interdisciplinary innovation and 
education. Corporate and University partners engage with the Center to 
generate human-centered solutions focused on deep research and exploratory 
development for local and global health challenges.
MATTER is a global healthcare startup incubator, community nexus and 
corporate innovation accelerator headquartered in Chicago. Since its inception 
in 2015, MATTER has supported over 1,000 healthcare and life sciences startups, 
fostering a community of 60,000 entrepreneurs, executives, investors, and 
experts dedicated to improving health and healthcare.
FEATURED INDUSTRY CLUSTERS

Agri-Tech and 
Food Procesing
The Center for Advanced Bioenergy and Bioproducts Innovation (CABBI), is 
a collaboration between the University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign and 20 
partner institutions. CABBI integrates recent advances in agronomics, genomics, 
and synthetic and computational biology to increase the value of energy crops — 
using a “plants as factories” approach to grow fuels and chemicals.
Integrated Bioprocessing Research Laboratory (IBRL) and its sister facility, the 
Food Science Human Nutrition Pilot Processing Plant, are pilot-scale facilities 
designed to help technologies in biomanufacturing, bioprocessing, and food 
processing de-risk at a commercially-relevant scale. Since opening in 2018, 
the facilities have worked with over 130 companies on their biomanufacturing 
technologies and successfully operate as a self-supporting model on the 
University of Illinois campus.
iFAB Tech Hub, drives growth in the precision fermentation and bioprocessing 
industry by linking assets, addressing gaps, and connecting infrastructure with 
wraparound programming to transform the region into a globally competitive 
precision fermentation lab-to-line
FEATURED INDUSTRY CLUSTERS

Quantum 
Computing, AI and 
Microelectronics
University of Illinois Research Park Urbana-Champaign: Research Park is an 
innovation hub for technology commercialization with a unique environment 
that cultivates startups and increases capabilities for established companies. 
Research Park has more than 120 companies and growing, employing over 2,100 
people in high-technology careers.
National Petascale Computing Facility, this 88,000-square-foot facility houses 
computing, networking, and data systems that benefit researchers. Offers top-
flight physical and cybersecurity, including monitoring of all exit bandwidth, deep 
packet inspection and tracking of network flows. All equipment in the facility 
must comply with cybersecurity and operational standards.
Holonyak Micro and Nanotechnology Lab, the University of Illinois Urbana-
Champaign HMNTL is one of the United States’ largest and most sophisticated 
facilities for conducting photonics, microelectronics, biotechnology, and 
nanotechnology research.
Illinois’ Rapidly Growing Quantum Ecosystem
Quantum technologies have the potential to tackle many of the biggest 
challenges in healthcare, energy, transportation, financial services, agriculture 
and national security. Illinois has become a leader in the emerging quantum 
computing industry thanks largely to the local Universities research efforts and 
state investments including the Illinois Quantum and Microelectronics Park 
(IQMP).
National Petascale 
Computing Facility
FEATURED INDUSTRY CLUSTERS

Transportation, 
Distribution, and Logistics 
CenterPoint Intermodal Center. America’s largest master-planned inland port. 
Positioned at the epicenter of the region’s transportation infrastructure. The 
campus has available warehouse and distribution center solutions, with rail 
access, interline service, and carload service for international and domestic 
intermodal capacity, including Free Trade Zone savings.
The Chicago Metropolitan Agency for Planning - CMAP is a regional government 
agency that works on behalf of the seven counties, 284 municipalities, and 8.6 
million residents of Northeastern Illinois. Through the CMAP and its Data Hub, 
communities access data snapshots on a variety of demographic, housing, 
employment, land use, freight, traffic, climate, and quality of life studies that 
inform its comprehensive planning to advance the regional transportation system 
for today and tomorrow.
Naper Settlement: Naper Settlement is an award-winning, outdoor history 
museum offering visitors 13 acres of educational programs, exhibits, guided 
tours, and special events. Whether watching the blacksmith at the forge or sitting 
down for a lesson in our one-room schoolhouse, visitors are immersed in history 
as they learn about the past and how it relates to the present.
FEATURED INDUSTRY CLUSTERS

Energy Production 
and Manufacturing 
Ecolab is a Global company offering advanced technology for water, hygiene, 
and infection prevention solutions and services. Ecolab’s leads science-based 
solutions, data-driven insights, and services to advance food safety, maintain 
clean and safe environments, and optimize water conservation and energy use 
across multiple industries.
FEATURED INDUSTRY CLUSTERS

Learned about economic development best practices that can be 
replicated.
Identified trade/investment opportunities to better position a 
company or institution.
Learned about business soft-landing opportunities in Illinois, the 
U.S., and/or in other countries.

Universidad Católica de Santiago de Guayaquil (Ecuador), will

2. Illinois Innovation Network (United States) and Invest 
Guatemala (Guatemala), will advance institutional 
cooperation.
3. Luhula Tradiciones de Mexico (Mexico) and Atzimba Catering 
Events (United States), to develop business partnerships of 
premium products from Luhula Tradiciones.
4. Luhula Tradiciones de Mexico (Mexico) and the Belize Trade 
and Investment Development Service BELTRAIDE (Belize), will 
cooperate in business opportunities in the creative and orange 
economy.
5. Radin Rahimzadeh (Entrepreneur, United States) and 
N7 Ventures (Brazil), will connect and develop innovation, 
mentoring and soft-landing opportunities for deep tech 
startups and investors in the United States and Brazil.
6. Government of Armenia and Radin Rahimzadeh 
(Entrepreneur, United States), will support the Armenian 
startup ecosystem. 
7. Government of Armenia and the Innovation Exchange 
Coalition (United States), will support the Armenian startup 
ecosystem. 
8. Province of Córdoba (Argentina) and the Innovation 
Exchange Coalition (United States), will support connecting 
startup ecosystems and accelerating tech startups in 
Argentina and the United States. 
MAIN RESULTS 
ON ACE ILLINOIS

9. Government of the Province of Córdoba (Argentina) and 
N7 Ventures (Brazil), will support startup ecosystem and 
knowledge exchange between the Brazilian and Argentinian 
startups. 
10. Enterprise Solved LLC (United States) and Business Works 
Limited (Jamaica), will contribute with knowledge exchange 
and support Jamaica’s SMEs.
 
11. Government of Santa Catarina, Nuevo Leon, (Mexico) and 
Illinois Innovation Network (United States), will support 
knowledge exchange and startup ecosystems.
12. Government of Santa Catarina, Nuevo Leon, (Mexico) and 
ThEEN (Germany), will cooperate in knowledge transfer 
and energy.
13. Government of Santa Catarina, Nuevo Leon, (Mexico) 
and Radin Rahimzadeh (Entrepreneur, United States), will 
develop startup ecosystem, growth, and investment.
14. Government of the Province of Tucumán (Argentina) and 
ThEEN (Germany), will cooperate in knowledge transfer 
and energy.
15. Invest Guatemala (Guatemala) and UpSquad (United 
States), will develop a pilot program to implement the 
UpSquad solution in Guatemala.
16. Business Works Limited (Jamaica) and Illinois State 
University for research (United States), will cooperate in 
educational, and professional programs.
17. UpSquad (United States) and the Belize Trade and 
Investment Development Service BELTRAIDE (Belize), will 
develop a pilot program to implement the UpSquad solution 
in Belize.

TESTIMONIALS
“Thanks to the ACE Program, I had the opportunity to connect with high-level decision-
makers from diverse industries and countries, many of whom represent potential 
partners to help advance our projects and strengthen our regional initiatives. This 
participation has opened doors to talented speakers or mentors or lobbyists who can 
contribute meaningfully to our programs to promote and grow entrepreneurship as 
part of our region’s economic development efforts. I’m currently following up with 
contacts such as Allison (mHub), Radin (USA), Danielle (Brazil), and Roberto (Ecuador) to 
collaborate and cross-promote our initiatives. Additionally, we’re excited to deepen our 
collaboration with Julia from Uruguay to highlight her story as a woman founder in tech—
her testimony will help shape a compelling narrative that can inspire our community.”
“We signed three letters of intent with international organizations that will allow us to 
develop strategies and initiatives for the economic growth of our city. This enables us 
to exchange best practices and information to continue improving our public policy on 
economic matters.”
“It has definitely improved capacities for economic development planning, learning about 
practices that a state promotes development, fosters advanced industries, and fosters 
an environment of collaboration among the entire ecosystem for the development 
of the state. It motivates the implementation of good practices in Guatemala. It also 
motivates people to think big about development in the country.”
Susana Lau Hou
Founder And CEO- Etyalab | IEEE Entrepreneurship VP
Etyalab | IEEE Entrepreneurship

Jorge Cervantes

Santa Catarina, Mexico
Evelyn Lisseth Córdova López

Ministry of Economy Guatemala 
Guatemala

“The ACE program has proven to be an invaluable platform to strengthen my work in 
legislative affairs, international cooperation, and inclusive economic development. It 
has allowed me to build new alliances, expand my global perspective, and identify 
practical avenues to translate ideas into action. For example, I followed up with 
Winston & Strawn LLP to gather their insights on the future of trade between Mexico, 
the United States, and Canada. Their perspective outside of Washington, D.C. enriches 
the Senate’s understanding and preparation for potential renegotiations of the trilateral 
agreement, providing a more diverse and informed foundation for legislative action. I 
also see myself as a facilitator between local and global actors.”
“The ACE program has been instrumental in advancing Healp’s [my startup] mission and 
expanding our strategic footprint across North America. Overall, the ACE program has 
significantly accelerated conversations with hospitals, academic research institutions, 
and international stakeholders, laying the foundation for long-term partnerships that 
align with Healp’s vision of equitable access to clinical research and patient-centered 
innovation.”
“The ACE program will contribute significantly to my work in several ways. First, it allowed 
me to connect with a broad network of international partners, opening up valuable 
opportunities for collaboration between companies. Second, I gained exposure to best 
practices that I can now recommend and adapt within my local projects. Finally, the 
program has strengthened my ability to continue promoting Medellín as a city full of 
potential and opportunities, leveraging the insights and connections gained during the 
experience.”
